One of these secrets that you're going to go to, okay? When I told you I'm going to write a successful letter for this guy's putter, guess what? See this? Here's what this letter says. Want to slash strokes from your game almost overnight. Amazing secret discovered by one-legged golfer adds 50 yards to your drives, eliminates hooks and slices, and can slash up to 10 strokes from your game almost overnight. Now you can learn to use your natural ability to load every drive with 200% more explosive power almost overnight getting distance you could only dream of before, while nailing shot after shot exactly where you want it as accurate as clockwork. And if you're like most golfers knocking a pile of strokes off your next round, impossible not if you believe what lifelong professionals and hot new amateurs worldwide are saying. First paragraph and I'm not going to read it all. Dear friend, if you've ever wanted to drive a golf ball with the explosive velocity of a howitzer cannon adding half the length of a football field to your tee shot alone while hitting all your fairways and greens with masterful precision then this will be the most important message you will ever read. Here's what it's all about. Guess what his putting letter is going to be modeled after. One of the things is when I wrote the letter that I just wrote to you I told you it took me 18 months. That's because I was making it up. One of the principles of marketing especially direct mail, honest to God, they were invented by me Gary Albert, struggling to invent them. You don't have to do that. Now we're going to take a Jeff Paul and someone in this room, I don't know who it is, some anal retentive dork is going to say to me but this letter is about increasing your drive, not your putting. Just go ahead and say it, whoever you are, say it, say it. How can I use this as a model? We are going to use this as a model. And the thing about writing things out to get the neurological imprint, the thing about having a good swipe file is not to swipe those things word for word. You don't have to do that. What you need is you need to be inspired by them, have a template from them and you know what I've got that none of you else have you had in the room? I've got, well you will have when you go home. How many letters are we giving them Terry? How many letters are we giving them? We promised 57 and we've got more in the package. Every single letter is a winner. There is nothing that you have to write about that you cannot find something to relate to. In this case we are going to relate to it directly. Now here is what I want you to do. This is a short letter. John Carlton and I work on things together. He has written this, by the way, John is now up to 18 consecutive winners in a row. He and I work together on these, 18 winners without a loser. Now we are going to break 90 minutes for lunch and during lunch I want you to read these two pages of this letter. Terry, we will see the young lady. Anna, could you stand up? Anna is easy to see. She is one of our South Beach models. She will help Terry pass. Would you stand up Anna so they can see you? They know who you are and you will help Terry pass the, she is going to pass them out. And then when we come back, do you all know what we are going to do, don't you? We are going to write this turkey a letter based on this. And it is not going to be the same, it is not going to be copyright infringement. We are going to use this as a model. My question to you is, why shouldn't we? This is running, yes? The whole curiosity is that template go on. What is the success of that letter? The template is running right now as an ad in golf digest and it is being mailed as a letter. John has three clients in Vizalia, California. He is doing it for and they almost can't get the money to the bank. That makes it a pretty good template, doesn't it? Is template bad? Yes, that is a good point. If your template is bad, actually you can have, the template could still be good. It could still be a very good template and your promotion might not work because you are not mailing it to a good list, right? Or you are not offering something that somebody wants. The key thing that you need to remember above all else is to make selling easy, you need to sell things that people want. Unless you are married emotionally to something, why not? Why not pick what people want? Golfers want to lower their score. Now I want you all to read this. Then we are going to come back, ask this man questions about his product and right in front of your eyes, we are going to create a winning promotion for him. Terry is going to type it, we are going to do the whole thing and it is going to be camera ready. Then we are going to dissect it so that you guys will all know how to do it for whatever it is that you are working on. What is it? It is one o'clock? One o'clock. Okay, now I am going to pick some people. Let me see here. Pierre, you are one. Anna, you are two. I am three. Eric, are you here? You are four. Hip, five. Jean Gowl, it would be six. I need somebody that looks anal retentive. Excuse me, what is your name? Right at the top of the shoulder. David? Are you talking about anal retentive? Yes, you are seven. This gentleman here in the plaid, you are eight. I need... Who else is here from the staff? Oh, lords, I am sorry Lorraine, you are nine. And... Look at this woman with this mean look on her face. See the one with the glasses there? Not the one with the... You are ten, okay? I would like all of you, instead of doing whatever you are doing for lunch, to let me buy lunch for you, which is already there in my room, which is what? Four oh one. Four oh one. So if you come up to four oh one, you actually will get a free lunch. You just won't get to pick what it is, because we just ordered a... Now all of you that I mentioned, would you please go to four oh one. The rest of you, the restaurant is set up for you, and we will be back here at two thirty. Okay? I also got a question. Did you want to use this? Yes, I do. If you just pass it around, they will pass. Yes sir. Please identify yourself for posterity. David Martin. You are sure? I am not. I am a little bit... Okay. Okay, then I am... So for the arm loader, do you have the arm strap with the whole thing, a little bit of a question? As I don't have a hard and fast rule, my suggestion is until you are very experienced that you do not do it. You can't teach, obviously in three days, you can't teach thirty years of copywriting. I can make an awfully big dent in it. So what I try and do is teach the safest thing that you can do. And you want to do the most low risk thing in everything that you do. And questions can be used as an opening paragraph or an opening sentence, but they are more risky than not using them. What about you? Same thing. Because you know, you see the danger? The danger is the answer could be no. Or who cares or no or they could turn it off. But I want to answer this question over here about long copy. And this may be one of the more important things that I teach you about writing copy. There has been four decades since the invention of advertising debates on long copy versus short copy. It's a ridiculous debate. You are either reading something that is interesting to you or you are not. Many decades ago a famous advertising man was challenged on this by a client. And the advertising man replied, look, I could write a full page in the New York Times and small type and guarantee you read every word of it. The client said no way. And he said, so they'd better steak dinner or something. And he said, okay, I'll prove it and all I'll do is write the headline. And the headline for the page in the New York Times is this page is all about John Q. Everett or whatever the guy's name is. Now if you picked up the paper and saw that headline and it was your name, what would you not read? Now I'm going to tell you how to think of this and this is crucially important. This is a crucial piece of information. I want you to imagine that whoever you're with now as a husband, spouse, girlfriend, significant other or what have you is no longer with you and you're going to replace that person. All right. And for some reason, maybe because he was bored, God appeared unto you. And he said, Buckwheat, I know that you need a wife or a new wife or a new husband. So I have decided to provide you with one and I have picked 100. Let's say you're a man. Well, in some cases, whatever it is. Okay. I'm just kidding. Let's let's say that your man and he got his picked 100 women and God said, just because I'm really bored. I'm going to make you choose the only woman that you will ever be with for the rest of your life from this collection of 100 women. And I'm not going to let you talk to them on the phone. I'm not going to let you see a photograph of them. I'm not going to let you see them on TV. I'm not going to let you talk to their neighbors. Nothing. All I'm going to ask is I'm going to have every one of those women write you a letter. And from those 100 letters, you pick the woman you spend the rest of your life with. What would you want her to tell you? Right. No, no, you don't let. Let's forget the picture for the moment because that. You did. No, quite seriously, would you want letters like hi, my name is bunny. I'm an aquarium and I like walks on the beach and rock and roll music. Hope you choose me. I don't think so. You'd want to know everything. Wouldn't you? How tall she was, how much you weighed what her religion was, what her age is, what her interests are, etc. Now, what if you are completely happy with the spouse that you are with? And you got this letter from this woman or this man describing themselves to you. How much of it would you read? Probably nothing. Why would you? The point here is this. The people who are, forget worrying about the people who aren't in the market or who aren't interested in what you're selling. You are not writing to them. Yes, it's true. Those people won't read long copy. A higher percentage of them will read short copy. You know? So what? What makes the difference? What you are trying to do is the same. And when it comes to something important, like choosing a name or a lot of things, the people who are prospects want to know everything. They don't want to know just a little bit. The people who are not prospects, yeah, more of them will read short copy because they can glance at it and read it. So don't leave anything out. Don't ever even let that be a consideration. Okay? Now, it turns out that Jeff Paul, who has the putter, already has a sales letter that he has written to sell this item. And he gave it to me to read. I took it up to the room and decided not to read it. I have not read it. We're going to go through it together so it will be a fresh experience for all of us. Now, there's something that I believe about writing that I have come to believe over the course of my career. I've come to learn. And I think it relates to why am I not rich, me personally. As a matter of fact, I have been rich several times and I suspect I will be again probably by the end of the year. The fact of the matter is it doesn't make much difference to me. Now, I don't mean that and money doesn't count. But money will not give you a lot of the things that you think it will if you've never had money. Money will solve the problems that money will solve. The late Joe Carvo, one of my best mentors and friends, said that you should make enough money until you have no more problems that money will solve. But the more significant thing that I found out through the course, especially of writing advertising and then writing my newsletter, is what a potent tool that is. Do you know that 25 years ago, I would fly in an airplane across the country and look down and realize that I had written words that made one out of every three homes that I have flown over. That I flew over, write a check to me or somebody that I had worked for. That was 25 years ago. I've probably caused more people to write a check and send it in the mail than anybody in history except the IRS. But when I started writing my newsletter, it added an entirely new dimension to what I was interested in. And, seminar. Terry Alleyon has worked with me for seven years now. And she's just about indispensable. And recently, for personal reasons relating to not her daughter and her schooling and everything, she had decided to move to Ocala, which is a small town in Florida. And she and I were still going to work together, but it would be by fax and phone and stuff like that. Well, I was preparing for that eventuality and something happened, sort of a blessing, and Terry is going to stay with me. And we have decided that to give up any pretense of us not working together, she's never going to find another boss like me because she's basically the boss. I'm never going to find another assistant like her. But one of the decisions that I've made and that she really enthusiastically endorses is I don't want to do anything anymore except teach. Nothing. I just want to write my newsletter and teach. I have an interest in other business ventures and some that look very lucrative and I want to give those to somebody, not sell them. I just want somebody to take them so they don't die. I'm going to be telling you all about some of those before the end of the seminar. I'm not selling them. I'm not charging anybody. Do you want these things? You go take it. Because with my newsletter, I make a very good living from my newsletter and it's very, very satisfying to me to write it. The stuff that we're going to do today is a little bit fun and I like showing off doing it, you know. But the truth of the matter is all it's going to do for Jeff Paul is bring in some more money. Now when I say all it's going to do, I don't mean cynical like that's not important, but this will be the 1189th time I've done this. I want to introduce you to a young woman. This is Lorraine Vicarro. Lorraine's 15 years old and she's a columnist for the Miami Herald. And her next article appears Wednesday. Wednesday. You're 15, right? Okay, now we're going to do something here. When I met her, I thought, wow, what a special kid. She's radiant when she smiles. It's like it lights up the room. She's gifted. She's intelligent. And she's in what, the Coral Gables or the Miami, the Dade School System. Give me a break. So I thought maybe with this young girl who's a gifted writer, I could get her mom to let her come to my seminar and I could get a hold of her before the carbonized shit is so dense that she's like all the rest of you people here, right? So what we're going to do is this letter about your putter. We're all going to participate in the writing of this, but I'm going to have Lorraine sit right out here and she's going to write it. This is going to be, I think, the first 15-year-old girl, person in history to write a top salesman. She didn't know I was going to do that. Don't be just crawling to the table there sitting there. Put a imagine in eye towel with a capital I. All the rest are small letters. Dot, dot, dot. I start falling in love with different things in my writing every once in a while. Right now some of you have noticed I'm in love with dots. I think they're called ellipses. You can write really long, wordy paragraphs and break them up. Imagine dot, dot, dot. Each of your strokes will be as accurate as clockwork. Anybody want to fault that? I'm asking too. Do you want to get in on pause at that point? No. No. And I want to explain why. If you are selling to the opportunity seeker market and you run ads like I do sometimes about my newsletter or about my book and I tell them very specifically what's in it as opposed to running a blind ad. Blind meaning they don't know what it is. The blind ad will almost always dramatically outpull the specific ad. Because in the opportunity seeker market people lose heart. As soon as they find out they have to do difficult things like get letterheads and business cards printed and apply for business license. Because they want it to be magic. No matter what it is, it's likely to disappoint. So I don't want to get specific until they've got an investment in this. I don't want the guy to be able to say at this point, hey, my pudding's okay. It's my driving that I need help with. Not yet, okay? Gary, it sounds like a general principle. Not your opportunity. Not always. Because sometimes people are so rabidly interested in what you're talking about. You want to get right to specifics. And in golf they are rabidly interested in it. But right now not everybody is interested in every part of their game equally at the same time. Yeah. Okay. Now we're down. Can you understand that? The thing is you don't want people to notice your writing. You want them to be moved by your message. Gary, I can't figure out how accurate and clockwork work together. Nor should you ever. We are, our job is to get this done. And trust me, we are going to get it done. Maybe not as impeccably well as it could be done, but we're going to settle for it having it done superbly well. Because that way we can keep moving. But there's a point where you have to, where you need to quit and you need to move on because then it becomes an excuse for inaction. There isn't a golfer in the world who would stop reading yet. All right. We're down to the salutation. Salutations are important. In this case, what you're reading is designed to be a space ad. We're going to make it a letter. The salutation is dear friend. In my opinion, unless you're actually using the man's name, you should never use anything except dear friend. Like dear fellow golfer. Because that makes someone stop and think, how did he know I was a fellow golfer? Dear fellow lover of the whales. Or what happened? Dear friend. It has a nice friendly ring to it. Dear friend.
